title: passions and the 5 stages of grief
subtitle: minus the acceptance

blocks.content: I have just finished my video.
My roommate is about to sleep. Not wanting to disturb him, I decide to go to the study room on our floor.
I close the lid of my laptop.
I walk to the study room.
I open up the laptop again. The preview of the exported video is gone. I must have closed it.
I try to make one final change to the video.
So I do what I have always done. I close CapCut.
CapCut loads up again, and I see the preview image of the video I’m working on. That’s odd. It’s an image of a clip I used when I first edited the video. One that I ended up deleting later.
Why would that be the preview image?
I click into the project. The project slowly loads. I look at the editing track.
The same 3 clips I started out with.
I feel my heart slowly begin to race. My mind races faster. It runs through all the possibilities at once, and it decides not to panic.
“You have the video exported,” it says.
“Worst case, you edit over the exported video.”

I click on the clip that was in the preview. I wait, as if somehow clicking that clip will make it disappear, and in exchange, it will return me to the row of expertly sliced clips that were in its place merely minutes ago.

We have reached the worst case, I decide. I open up my files. I begin to search. I blindly start going through folders. At first, they are ones that the exported video might plausibly be in. Very soon, they turn into folders like “School”, “Debate”, and “Rental Documents”.
I reach a moment of clarity. A brief spike of relief. Obviously, the video won’t be in these folders. There’s a better way to find them. I type in the name of the video.
The first result is a folder. I look inside it. It’s filled with all the clips I used to make the video.
I check another result. It has .mp4 after it. It’s an audio file of the voiceover I did for the video.
I check the next result. Another audio file. I had recorded a better voiceover.
It has a .mov after it. It’s a video file.
I remember that I recorded the voiceover as a video and extracted the audio.
I begin to look into random folders again, every so often searching for the name of the video, and looking through those 4 results.
“capcut deleted my video. I was working on it. I exported it. I even played it. And now I can’t find it at all.”
I scan through the solutions it gives me. I have done them all.
I continue looking through random folders.
At some point, I go back to Deepseek.
I type: *“be honest, if I exported it and even played the whole thing, does it exist on my laptop guaranteed”*
It replies: *“Honest answer: No, it is not guaranteed.”*
I type again: *“how could capcut just delete something even after it exported it for me”*
At the end it says “How To Make Sure This Never Happens Again”.

Reality begins to hit as I walk back to my dorm room, defeated. I think of all the school tasks I put off for the video. The reworked schedule of tasks that I know deep down I won’t be able to complete.
I crawl into bed and drift off to sleep.
I feel normal for a second. And then the thought immediately pierces me again.
I try to go back to sleep. But my mind starts to turn. It took me 8 hours in total to make the video. But that included planning, finding the clips I needed, and thinking about how to edit the video.
If I were to do it again…
I knew exactly how I wanted the editing to look.
I could finish a lot faster.
I open my eyes. I get out of bed. I put on my sweater and pants. I grab my laptop. And I go back to the study room.
I finish the second version of the video at around 9 in the morning. 7 hours after I realized the original version got deleted.
As I upload the video, I realize something:
I really do love editing videos.
